coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen, sulfur co-doped red carbon dots for sensitive and selective detection of Sn2+ ions
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Peiyuan
Zhu, Zhijun
Li, Zhenhua
Xu, Yanan
Belfiore, Laurence A.
Tang, Jianguo
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots with both red-emission and red-excitation (R-CDs) have attracted much attention due to their excellent optical property and promising application in various fields. However, most carbon dots (CDs) with red-emission are only excited with short wavelength irradiation, which heavily hinders their further applications. Herein, novel R-CDs are obtained through a hydrothermal synthesis by using p-phenylenediamine and ammonium thiocyanate as precursors. The as-synthesized R-CDs exhibit a strong red emission at 618 nm at a long excitation (598 nm). Moreover, the R-CDs are explored as a selective and sensitive “turn-off” sensor for Sn2+ detection by the non-radiative recombination of the excitons through effective electron, charge or energy transfer between Sn2+ and surface functional groups such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, amine, thiophene, and SO, with a detection range from 0 to 90 μM and a detection limit of as low as 17.25 nM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Heteroatoms doping
Red excitation and emission
Detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of the N,S-RCDs towards metal ions

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by (1) the Open Funds of the State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry (SKLEAC201906); (2) the Natural Scientific Foundation of China (Grant No. 51473082 ); (3) State Key Project of International Cooperation Research ( 2017YFE0108300 , 2016YFE0110800 ); (4) Shandong Double-Hundred Project (2018); (5) the National Plan for Introducing Talents of Discipline to Universities (“111” plan) ; (6) The 1st Class Discipline Program of Shandong Province of China .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM (a and b) and HR-TEM (c) images of N,S-RCDs. Inset of panel a is the particle size distribution of N,S-RCDs. (d) XRD pattern of the N,S-RCDs powders obtained by freeze-drying.
(a) FT-IR spectrum of N,S-RCDs. (b) XPS survey spectrum of as-prepared N,S-RCDs. XPS spectra of C1s (c), N1s (d), O1s (e), and S2p (f) spices on N,S-RCDs, respectively.

(a) UV–Vis absorption, excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) spectra of resultant N,S-RCDs in aqueous solution; Inset: photographs of N,S-RCDs aqueous solution under ambient condition (left) and irradiated with a 550 nm light (right), respectively. (b) PL spectra of N,S-RCDs excited at different wavelengths.

a. Comparison of PL intensity of the N,S-RCDs (0.2 mg/mL) after adding Sn2+ (70 μM) and other different ions (700 μM). b. PL intensity of N,S-RCDs (0.2 mg/mL) in the presence of Sn2+ ions (0, 0.02, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 10, 20, 40, 60, 70, 80, 90 μM).c. The change of PL intensity of N,S-RCDs solution versus the concentration of Sn2+ (1–90 μM); Inset of panel c is the change of PL intensity of N,S-RCDs solution versus the concentration of Sn2+ (1–100 nM). d. PL lifetime decays of N,S-RCDs in the presence and absence of Sn2+.
Comparison of different fluorescent probes for Sn2+ detection.

row.entry: Rhodamine B derivative linked with ethylenediamine (R1)
Rhodamine B derivative linked with tert-butyl carbazate group (R2)
carbon nanoparticles from waste rice husk

head.ce:title: Nitrogen, sulfur co-doped red carbon dots for sensitive and selective detection of Sn2+ ions

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Center of International Research for Hybrid Materials Technology
National Base of International Science & Technology Cooperation
College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots with both red-emission and red-excitation (R-CDs) have attracted much attention due to their excellent optical property and promising application in various fields. However, most carbon dots (CDs) with red-emission are only excited with short wavelength irradiation, which heavily hinders their further applications. Herein, novel R-CDs are obtained through a hydrothermal synthesis by using p-phenylenediamine and ammonium thiocyanate as precursors. The as-synthesized R-CDs exhibit a strong red emission at 618 nm at a long excitation (598 nm). Moreover, the R-CDs are explored as a selective and sensitive “turn-off” sensor for Sn2+ detection by the non-radiative recombination of the excitons through effective electron, charge or energy transfer between Sn2+ and surface functional groups such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, amine, thiophene, and SO, with a detection range from 0 to 90 μM and a detection limit of as low as 17.25 nM.

ce:section.ce:para: CDs have received increasing attention and enormous applications due to their unique optical property, simple synthesis route, wide raw sources, good biocompatibility and excellent solubility in most solvents [1–4]. However, most CDs are excited with and even emit green or blue light, which greatly limits their further applications in the fields of biomedical theranostics, light harvesting and photocatalysis [5,6]. Therefore, R-CDs with longer wavelength excitation and emission are highly desired for some applications.

So far, numerous studies are trying to synthesize R-CDs using different precursors [6]. Chemicals with aromatic structure and heteroatoms have been regarded as the most promising candidate precursor, because lower energy gap would be introduced by generating large sp2 domains to tune the excitation and emission to longer wavelength [7]. In addition, the well-defined molecular structure could contribute to reveal the photoluminescence (PL) mechanism by providing controlled surface chemistry [8,9]. Phenylenediamines (PDA) have been used as precursors for the synthesis of CDs with red emission and/or excitation [10]. Sun et al. reported red-emitting CDs through a one-step microwave-assisted treatment of p-phenylenediamine (pPDA) dissolved in the mixture of ethanol and water, and the resulted CDs exhibit longwave emission (620 nm) under a 470 nm excitation [11]. Liu et al. prepared red emitting CDs through hydrothermal treatment of o-phenylenediamine (oPDA) with HNO3, which emitted optimal emission at around 630 nm under various excitations [12]. In general, the large conjugated rigid sp2 domains generated by phenylenediamine or the recombination of electron-holes of the excited carriers trapped by the surface states result in the red emission [13,14]. Furthermore, these heteroatoms come from carbon sources or are added to CDs through small molecule forms, which can change the original internal structure or surface functional groups of CDs, thus changing the wavelength of emission and improving the QY [15]. Therefore, it is highly desirable and a great challenge for new R-CDs based on abundant heterogeneous elements.

Sn2+ has been reported as an essential trace element for human, which has important influence on people to carry out physiological activities and maintain the health of human. Sn2+ was found to effectively protect against erosive tooth wear and found accumulated in human organs such as the adrenal gland, liver, brain and spleen, which is involved in growth factors and cancer prevention of human beings [16–18]. The deficiency of Sn2+ may lead to dysplasia and hearing loss, but excessive accumulation of tin can adversely affect the respiratory and digestive systems [19]. In addition, Sn2+ is usually applied to label 99mTc in radiopharmaceutical cold kits as a specific chemical reaction ligand and used as preservatives in food [20,21]. Therefore, it is of great importance to detect Sn2+ with a high sensitivity and selectivity. Traditional techniques including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [22], anodic stripping voltammetry (AVS) [23], and colorimetric detection [24] have some disadvantages such as requiring expensive and sophisticated instruments, low selectivity, and time-consuming detection process, which limits the general Sn2+ detection. Fluorescence probe have been proven to be a suitable method for Sn2+ detection as it offers significant advantages of high sensitivity and selectivity, quick response, and easy availability [25–27].

In this work, a novel kind of N and S co-doped R-CDs (N,S-RCDs) with efficient red emission at longwave excitation was proposed by using pPDA and ammonium thiocyanate (NH4SCN) as precursors. Moreover, the N,S-RCDs have been explored as a probe of Sn2+ with high selectivity and sensitivity through the strong interaction between the various surface functional groups and Sn2+.

Ammonium thiocyanate (NH4SCN) was purchased from Aladdin Company. p-phenylenediamine (pPDA) was obtained from Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). Stannous chloride dihydrate (SnCl2·2H2O) was bought from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. Tris-HCl, PBS, HEPES buffer solution was purchased from Solarbio Company. NaCl, KCl, LiCl, NiCl2, ZnCl2, BaSO4, MgCl2, CaSO4, PbCl2, FeCl2, FeCl3, CoCl2, CuCl2, Hg(NO3)2, K2SO4, KNO3 and ethanol absolute (>99.7%) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. (Beijing, China). All reagents are of analytical grade and were used without further treatment.

The UV–Vis (UV–Vis) absorption spectrum was recorded in a Unico (type 4802) UV–Vis–NIR spectrophotometer. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images were recorded on a transmission microscope (JEM-2100HR, JEOL) operating at 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis was performed using Thermo escalab XPS instrument (250xi). Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were recorded on a Rigaku D/MAX 2550 diffractometer with Cu Ka radiation (k = 1.5418 Å). Photoluminescence (PL) analysis was performed on FLS1000 Photoluminescence Spectrometer, which is used to obtain excitation spectrum, emission spectrum, fluorescence lifetime, and PL efficiency for solid, liquid, powder and film samples in UV–Vis–NIR range. FT-IR analysis was performed using a Centaur Model 10X Nexus 670 (FTIR) with a MCT-B detector with a spectral range of 4,000 to 400 cm−1.

N,S-RCDs were facilely prepared by hydrothermally treating pPDA and NH4SCN. Briefly, 0.054 g of pPDA and 0.38 g of NH4SCN with the molar ratio of 1:10 were dissolved in 5 mL of ultrapure water and stirred for 20 min to form a uniform mixture solution in a nitrogen atmosphere. Then, the aqueous solution was transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-equipped stainless autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the deep red products were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min to remove the sediment. For further purification, unreacted small molecules were removed by dialyzing for 24 h. Finally, the deep red solid-state N,S-RCDs were obtained by freeze-drying. The purified N,S-RCDs were further dispersed in ethanol for further using and the final concentration of solution was 20 mg/mL.

N,S-RCDs (30 μL, 20 mg/mL) was diluted by 0.01 M Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH = 7.4, 3 ml) for excluding the effect of pH change on fluorescence. The fluorescence signals of the N,S-RCDs solution with different concentrations of Sn2+ were recorded on the spectrophotometer with excitation wavelength at 550 nm after reacting for 10 min at room temperature. Besides, the selectivity of the detection system was examined by various metal ions and anions, including Na+, K+, Li+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, SO2-4, Cl− and NO-3 under the identical condition.

TEM and HR-TEM were employed to characterize the morphology and structure of the as-prepared N,S-RCDs. As shown in Fig. 1 a–b, the nearly spherical particles with uniform size distributions of an average diameter of 2.29 nm can be directly observed, which could effectively minimize the steric hindrance effect [28]. Such small nanoparticles was well dispersed in various solvents (Figs. S1a–d). The HR-TEM image reveals the high crystalline structure of the N,S-RCDs with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to (100) lattice spacing of graphite, which demonstrates the highly crystalline graphite-like structure of N,S-RCDs (Fig. 1c). The XRD pattern in Fig. 1d shows a wider diffraction peak centered at 21.5°, indicating the partial graphitization and the interlayer spacing of the (002) diffraction peak of 0.41 nm [29]. The larger interlayer spacing than that of graphite (0.34 nm) could be attributed to the introduction of N-containing and S-containing groups in the synthesis process [30]. In addition, the small size of N,S-RCDs and the amorphous nature on the surface of N,S-RCDs contribute to the broad diffraction peak width [31,32].

The elemental composition and the functional groups on the surface of the N,S-RCDs were characterized through the FT-IR and XPS spectroscopy. The broad vibration bands at ∼3313, ∼3190, and ∼3050 cm−1 (Fig. 2 a) were ascribed to stretching vibrations of O–H, N–H and C–H, respectively. The sharp peaks at 1603, 1398, and 1315 cm−1 correspond to the vibrations of CN/CC, C–N = /C–N–C and C–NH, indicating the existence of nitrogenous ring structure [33,34]. Furthermore, the absorption bands in the range of 1085–1225 cm−1 are attributed to stretching vibrations of C–O and C–OH groups, confirming the oxygen-rich property of the N,S-RCDs [35]. Besides, the peaks at 822 and 617 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration of SO and C–S–C, respectively [36,37]. The XPS survey spectrum in Fig. 2b reveals that the N,S-RCDs are mainly composed of C (61.67%), N (13.76%), O (10.87%) and S (13.70%) elements. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (Fig. 2c) indicates the presence of C–C/CC(284.5 eV), CO (285.2 eV), and C–N/C–O/C–S(286.1 eV). Typically, the peak at 284.5 eV is assigned to sp2 domains, while the peak at 286.1 eV is ascribed to sp3 domains. The high resolution XPS spectrum of N1s (Fig. 2d) showed four peaks at 398.9, 399.5, and 400.8 eV, which are attributed to pyridinic N, graphite N, and N-H, respectively. The two peaks at 531.6 and 532.6 eV in the deconvolution of the O1s spectrum (Fig. 2e) are assigned to C–O and CO groups, respectively. Besides, the XPS spectrum of S2p (Fig. 2f) shows two fitted peaks of S2p3/2 (164.1eV) and S2p1/2 (165.1 eV) eV, attributed to the C–S–C covalent bond of thiophene-type sulfur owning to the spin–orbit splitting [37,38]. Then, two minor peaks of S2p3/2 (168.4 eV) and S2p1/2 (169.4 eV) are consistent with –C–S(O)2 –C– sulfone bridges [39]. Such results demonstrate that sulfur is primarily doped at the edges and defects of the N,S-RCDs surface in the form of SO functional group and thiophene motif. The FT-IR and XPS results confirm that the functional groups such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, amine, thiophene, and SO coexist on the surface of the N and S co-doped RCDs. These functional groups endow the N,S-RCDs the hydrophilicity and stability in an aqueous system, which implies a great potential as a fluorescent probe in aqueous solution.

To investigate the optical properties of the N,S-RCDs, UV–vis absorption and PL spectra of the N,S-RCDs were recorded. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the optimal emission peak at 618 nm was observed when excited at 598 nm, corresponding to the strong absorption peak at 598 nm, indicating the large sp2 π-conjugation domains in the N and S-rich multilayer graphene-like cores of N,S-RCDs. The absorption bands in the region of 300–800 nm are attributed to the π–π* transition of graphite sp2 domains and n–π* transitions by the surface states [40]. Such unique absorption features endow the CDs with red emission characteristics. As it can be seen in Fig. 3b, the maximum emission peak at 618 nm is virtually independent of the excitation wavelengths varying from 400 to 600 nm. The excitation-independent FL behavior indicates that the surface of N,S-RCDs has a relatively uniform surface state [41]. Furthermore, the quantum yield (QY) of N,S-RCDs was measured to be 17.58% in ethanol and 8.7% in water under 598 nm excitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of the N,S-RCDs towards metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared N,S-RCDs were then explored as a detection probe of Sn2+ in aqueous solutions at room temperature. As shown in Fig. S2, under the 2 μg/mL concentration of N,S-RCDs, the highest sensitivity of Sn2+ was found in Tris-HCl buffer solution over HEPES and PBS buffer solution. Therefore, the following experiments were carried out in Tris-HCl buffer solution.

The corresponding PL intensity ratio of F0/F (F0 and F are fluorescence intensities of N,S-RCDs at 618 nm in the absence and presence of metal ions, respectively) was employed to evaluate the responses of this probe to different ions. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the highest F0/F value was observed in the presence of Sn2+ (70 μM) over other ions with a much higher concentration of 700 μM under the identical detection condition, indicating the extremely high selectivity of N,S-RCDs toward Sn2+ ions.To further investigate the sensitivity of N,S-RCDs probe, the PL spectra were measured in the presence of Sn2+ with different concentrations. As shown in Fig. 4b, the PL intensity obviously decreased with the increase of Sn2+ concentration in a wide range of 0–90 μM, indicating that the addition of Sn2+ ions can effectively quench the fluorescence of the N,S-RCDs. In Fig. 4c, the quenching efficiency (F0/F) exhibited a good linear relationship versus the concentration of Sn2+ in the range of 1–100 nM and 1–90 μM. The fluorescence quenching data were calculated using the Stern-Volmer equation:
F0/F = 1 + Ksv[Q]
where Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant; [Q] is the concentration of quencher. In the range of 1–100 nM, the linear calibration curve can be expressed as F0/F = 1.0023 + 0.0017[Sn2+], and the linear correlation coefficient (R2) is 0.997. The limit of detection (LOD) for Sn2+ was obtained to be 17.25 nM based on a 3σ/S (a signal-to-noise ratio of 3), where σ represents the standard deviation of 20 blank measurements, and S is the slope of the calibration curve [42]. In the concentration range of 1–90 μM, the linear calibration curve can be expressed as F0/F = 1.06 + 0.0426[Sn2+] with a R2 value of 0.992. As shown in Table 1 , it should be noted that the obtained LOD of the N,S-RCDs sensor of Sn2+ was much lower than those previously reported comparing with other different fluorescent probes.

Generally, quenching process refers to the interaction between the luminophore and the quenching molecule, which induces the decay of fluorescence intensity via static quenching or dynamic quenching. As we know, it is one of the most effective methods to distinguish static quenching from dynamic quenching by measuring fluorescence lifetime in the presence and absence of quenching molecules [42]. As shown in Fig. 4d, the PL lifetime decays of N,S-RCDs in the presence and absence of Sn2+ were measured. The almost constant fluorescence lifetime values of N,S-RCDs is 2.19 ns and can be fitted into two components: 0.705 ns (35.04%) and 2.99 ns (64.96%). In the presence of Sn2+ ions (100 μM), the Sn2+-N,S-RCDs lifetime decreased to 1.08 ns, of which decay time can also be fitted into two components: 0.668 ns (81.2%) and 2.85 ns (18.8%). The decreased lifetime indicates that there is a dynamic quenching process in the Sn2+-N,S-RCDs system [37]. In addition, the quenching mechanism can be clarified by the comparison of the absorption spectrum of the fluorophore before and after quenching [45]. To further explain the quenching mechanism of N,S-RCDs, the UV–vis absorption spectra of Sn2+, N,S-RCDs and the Sn2+-N,S-RCDs system were recorded, respectively. As shown in Fig. S3, the UV–vis absorption spectra of N,S-RCDs and the Sn2+-N,S-RCDs are almost identical, indicating a dynamic quenching mechanism of the Sn2+-N,S-RCDs. On the other hand, the N,S-RCDs can selectively interact with Sn2+ ions due to various of functional groups such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, amine, thiophene, and SO coexisting on the surface of the as-prepared N,S-RCDs. These functional groups containing N, O, and S on CDs may improve their ability of chelation with metal ions and accelerate the non-radiative recombination of the excitons, further leading to fluorescence quenching [46,47].

In summary, we report a novel and facile route to synthesize N,S-RCDs with both red-emission and red-excitation by using pPDA and NH4SCN as precursors. Owing to introducing N and S atoms into CDs and forming some new function groups such as amine, thiophene, and SO, the obtained N,S-RCDs exhibit a strong red emission (618 nm). Moreover, The fluorescence of N,S-RCDs are successfully applied for sensitive and selective detection of Sn2+ with a detection limit as low as 17.25 nM, which is attributed to non-radiative recombination of the excitons through effective electron, charge or energy transfer between N,S-RCDs and Sn2+. Finally, the present strategy provides a new strategy for developing low-cost and selective sensors for detection of Sn2+ in aqueous solution.

Peiyuan Zhu: Validation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Zhijun Zhu: Supervision, Conceptualization, Editing, Data curation, Funding acquisition. Zhenhua Li: Co-experiments. Yanan Xu: Co-experiments. Laurence A. Belfiore: Validation. Jianguo Tang: Supervision, Editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by (1) the Open Funds of the State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry (SKLEAC201906); (2) the Natural Scientific Foundation of China (Grant No. 51473082); (3) State Key Project of International Cooperation Research (2017YFE0108300, 2016YFE0110800); (4) Shandong Double-Hundred Project (2018); (5) the National Plan for Introducing Talents of Discipline to Universities (“111” plan); (6) The 1st Class Discipline Program of Shandong Province of China.
